title: Ordering Chai & Street Food
situation: You're at a roadside chai stall with a friend. You want chai and some snacks.

phrases.context: 'dena' = give — basic way to order anything
'wali' = the one with — incredibly versatile word

phrases.hindi: ek plate momos bhi laga do
phrases.english: Put one plate of momos too
phrases.context: 'laga do' = serve/put — common at food stalls
Ask this when you're done — means 'what's the bill?'
Always ask before handing a big note

grammar_notes: 'dena' (give) is the universal ordering verb at food stalls
'milega/milegi?' = will I get? (male/female noun) — polite way to ask availability
Numbers: ek (1), do (2), teen (3), chaar (4), paanch (5)
culture_notes: At street stalls, you don't wait to be served — just call out your order
Chai is always shared — offering chai = offering friendship
'Cutting chai' in Mumbai = half cup, perfect for a quick break

skill_breakdown.explanation: Basic numbers: ek(1), do(2), teen(3), chaar(4), paanch(5), chhe(6), saat(7), aath(8), nau(9), das(10). For ordering: number + item + dena.
'Wala/wali/wale' = the one who/which/with. Attach to anything: noun+wala = the one with that thing. Incredibly flexible — you can describe anything.

more_examples.english: the paneer burger (the burger with paneer)

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Dena' (give), 'rakhna' (keep), 'banana' (make) — use these verb roots + what you want. Add 'zara' (just/please) for extra politeness.

more_examples.english: heat it up a bit more

practice_prompt: You're at a chai stall near your office. You want one special chai (less sugar, extra ginger) and one plate of bread pakora. Your friend wants cold coffee. Order for both of you, ask the price, and deal with the guy not having change for 500 rupees.
